policy_statement: NAP target: energy production/transmission/distribution companies with adaptation or contingency plans for extreme events to reach 25% by 2020 and 100% by 2030.
mechanism_description: Sets targets for energy production, transmission, and distribution companies to have adaptation or contingency plans for extreme events, improving power system resilience to climate-related disruptions.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard (extreme events) → preparedness/operational changes in power system → ↓ outage likelihood/duration → ↓ exposure & disruption (↑ resilience)
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Changes utility behavior by set/targeting contingency and adaptation planning for generation, transmission, and distribution (e.g., emergency procedures, redundancy, staffing, spares, mutual aid, prioritized restoration). (2) These plans improve operational readiness and system robustness, enabling preventive actions and faster fault isolation/restoration during storms, floods, heatwaves, or wildfires. (3) The outcome is reduced climate-related service interruption and quicker recovery, improving energy-system resilience and reducing societal and economic impacts from climate hazards.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced outage risk and faster recovery of electricity supply during climate-related extreme events, lowering exposure and disruption for people and critical services.
mechanism_classification_reasoning: 1) Dominant causal pathway: climate extremes threaten grid assets/operations; contingency planning increases preparedness and continuity, reducing exposure to disruption (adaptation). 2) Chosen over mitigation/resource efficiency because the main intended and typical effect is reliability under hazards, not reduced energy use or GHG emissions; any emissions effects (e.g., fewer outage-related generator use) are secondary. 3) Edge cases: if a “resilience plan” primarily funds efficiency upgrades or low-carbon generation, those would be separate mechanisms; here the defining feature is extreme-event planning for continuity. 4) Applicability check: all policies sharing this mechanism (utility extreme-event contingency/adaptation planning) fundamentally aim to manage climate risk and maintain service, which is adaptation across contexts. 5) Secondary subtype (e.g., critical infrastructure resilience, emergency management) is deferred to policy-level evidence and not assigned at the mechanism level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some contingency plans may include hardening or distributed energy resources that can also reduce emissions; however, the dominant mechanism remains reduced hazard-related outages (adaptation).
key_indicators: “have adaptation plans or contingency plans for extreme events”; “25%”; “100%”
co_benefits: Improved non-climate emergency preparedness and reliability (e.g., for technical failures/accidents), reduced economic disruption from outages
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be interpreted as a regulatory mandate if the NAP target is embedded in binding licensing/compliance rules, but the provided text only evidences a coverage target (percent of companies with plans), not an enforceable obligation or performance standard.
